paper.title: Attention is All you Need

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

tldr.text: A new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely is proposed, which generalizes well to other tasks by applying it successfully to English constituency parsing both with large and limited training data.

paper.abstract: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks in an encoder-decoder configuration. The best performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train. Our model achieves 28.4 BLEU on the WMT 2014 English-to-German translation task, improving over the existing best results, including ensembles by over 2 BLEU. On the WMT 2014 English-to-French translation task, our model establishes a new single-model state-of-the-art BLEU score of 41.8 after training for 3.5 days on eight GPUs, a small fraction of the training costs of the best models from the literature. We show that the Transformer generalizes well to other tasks by applying it successfully to English constituency parsing both with large and limited training data.

arguments.query: "Attention is All you Need" limitations critique

result.prefix: {"papers": [{"paperId": "5dc15ac1c92ab7492f121471823fb13a95d273ba", "title": "A Mechanistic Interpretation of Arithmetic Reasoning in Language Models using Causal Mediation Analysis", "year": 2023, "citationCount": 74, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "https://aclanthology.org/2023.emnlp-main.435.pdf", "status": "HYBRID", "license": "CCBY", "disclaimer": "Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.15054, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "authors": [{"authorId": "2175480389", "name": "Alessandro Stolfo"}, {"authorId": "2083259", "name": "Yonatan Belinkov"}, {"authorId": "2790926", "name": "Mrinmaya Sachan"}], "abstract": "Mathematical reasoning in large language models (LMs) has garnered significant attention in recent work, but there is a limited understanding of how these models process and store information related to arithmetic tasks within their architecture. In order to improve our understanding of this aspect of language models, we present a mechanistic interpretation of Transformer-based LMs on arithmetic questions using a causal mediation analysis framework. By intervening on the activations of specific model components and measuring the resulting changes in predicted probabilities, we identify the subset of parameters responsible for specific predictions. This provides insights into how information related to arithmetic is processed by LMs. Our experimental results indicate that LMs process the input by transmitting the information relevant to the query from mid-sequence early layers to the final token using the attention mechanism. Then, this information is processed by a set of MLP modules, which generate result-related information that is incorporated into the residual stream. To assess the specificity of the observed activation dynamics, we compare the effects of different model components on arithmetic queries with other tasks, including number retrieval from prompts and factual knowledge questions."}, {"paperId": "b553f54f03600e5a9ab8893e26c841f287a58889", "title": "Causal Mediation Analysis: A Summary\u2010Data Mendelian Randomization Approach", "year": 2025, "citationCount": 5, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "", "status": null, "license": "CCBY", "disclaimer": "Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11799828, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "authors": [{"authorId": "2242527244", "name": "Shu-Chin Lin"}, {"authorId": "5692645", "name": "Sheng-Hsuan Lin"}, {"authorId": "2339458559", "name": "Tian Ge"}, {"authorId": "2251676311", "name": "Chia-Yen Chen"}, {"authorId": "2242599076", "name": "Yen-Feng Lin"}], "abstract": "Summary\u2010data Mendelian randomization (MR), a widely used approach in causal inference, has recently attracted attention for improving causal mediation analysis. Two existing methods corresponding to the difference method and product method of linear mediation analysis have been developed to perform MR\u2010based mediation analysis using the inverse\u2010variance weighted method (MR\u2010IVW). Despite these developments, there is still a need for more rigorous, efficient, and precise MR\u2010based mediation methodologies. In this study, we develop summary\u2010data MR\u2010based frameworks for causal mediation analysis. We improve the accuracy, statistical efficiency and robustness of the existing MR\u2010based mediation analysis by implementing novel variance estimators for the mediation effects, deriving rigorous procedures for statistical inference, and accounting for widespread pleiotropic effects. Specifically, we propose Diff\u2010IVW and Prod\u2010IVW to improve upon the existing methods and provide the pleiotropy\u2010robust methods (Diff\u2010Egger, Diff\u2010Median, Prod\u2010Egger, and Prod\u2010Median), adapted from MR\u2010Egger and MR\u2010Median, to enhance the robustness of the MR\u2010based mediation analysis. We conduct comprehensive simulation studies to compare the existing and proposed methods. The results show that the proposed methods, Diff\u2010IVW and Prod\u2010IVW, improve statistical efficiency and type I error control over the existing approaches. Although all IVW\u2010based methods suffer from directional pleiotropy biases, the median\u2010based methods (Diff\u2010Median and Prod\u2010Median) can mitigate such biases. The differences among the methods can lead to discrepant statistical conclusions as demonstrated in real data applications. Based on our simulation results, we recommend the three proposed methods in practice: Diff\u2010IVW, Prod\u2010IVW, and Prod\u2010Median, which are complementary under various scenarios."}, {"paperId": "f15a193c93c0ff4fae305c2af6a67b8aab2f7011", "title": "Adverse childhood experiences mediate the association between ADHD symptoms and severe psychological distress in the Japanese general population: a causal mediation analysis of 29,268 participants", "year": 2025, "citationCount": 0, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "", "status": null, "license": "CCBYNCND", "disclaimer": "Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12752057, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "authors": [{"authorId": "46371656", "name": "Keita Tokumitsu"}, {"authorId": "4330901", "name": "Norio Sugawara"}, {"authorId": "2283048245", "name": "T. Tabuchi"}, {"authorId": "1397046305", "name": "N. Yasui-Furukori"}], "abstract": "Adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) include various forms of trauma or chronic stress during childhood, e.g., physical, emotional, and sexual abuse; neglect; and household dysfunction. ACEs have been linked to long-term psychological difficulties. Emerging research suggests that individuals with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) may be more frequently exposed to ACEs due to familial conflict or behavioral dysregulation. However, it remains unclear which specific ACE types mediate the association between ADHD symptoms and adult psychological distress. We analyzed data from 29,268 adults in a nationwide Japanese internet-based survey. ADHD symptoms were measured using the Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (ASRS-J-6), and severe psychological distress (SPD) was measured using the Kessler Psychological Distress Scale (K6) scale. Participants reported exposure to ten types of ACEs. Causal mediation analysis was performed to identify ACEs that mediated the ADHD\u2013SPD association, adjusting for demographic variables. Among participants, 3.69% screened positive for ADHD symptoms, 12.81% met the criteria for SPD, and 5.09% reported four or more ACEs. Both ADHD symptoms and greater ACE exposure were significantly associated with SPD. Five ACE indicators, including emotional neglect, emotional abuse, physical abuse, household physical violence, and having four or more ACEs, significantly mediated the ADHD\u2013SPD relationship. Emotional neglect had the strongest mediating effect (11.19%). Emotion-related ACEs partially mediate the association between ADHD symptoms and adult psychological distress. While preventing childhood adversity remains essential, addressing ADHD symptoms through psychoeducation and intervention may reduce psychological burden in adulthood. These findings underscore the importance of trauma-informed approaches in the assessment and treatment of individuals with ADHD. Not applicable."}, {"paperId": "982e61a8c9326942eb52fd3eb76a483b2fce2947", "title": "Survival Analysis Under the Aalen's Additive Hazards Model With Covariate Measurement Error: Application to Causal Mediation Analysis", "year": 2025, "citationCount": 0, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "", "status": null, "license": null, "disclaimer": "Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1002/sim.70346?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1002/sim.70346, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "authors": [{"authorId": "1475524726", "name": "Xialing Wen"}, {"authorId": "2400059759", "name": "Liangchen Qin"}, {"authorId": "2365266137", "name": "Huijun Wu"}, {"authorId": "2306096040", "name": "Ying Yan"}], "abstract": "Covariate measurement error is an important problem in survival analysis, which has been well studied under the Cox proportional hazards model. However, measurement error effects have been rarely addressed under the Aalen's additive hazards model, and there is a lack of methods to correct for error effects. In recent years, the Aalen's additive hazards model has been increasingly used in causal mediation analysis. Although the longitudinal mediator is frequently measured with uncertainty, the issue of measurement error in the mediator has received little attention. In this article, we study the general problem of covariate measurement error under the Aalen's additive hazards model and propose a measurement error correction strategy. We then extend the proposed method to causal mediation analysis in the survival setting with an error\u2010prone longitudinal mediator. Corrected estimation of the direct and indirect effects is obtained. The performance of the proposed method is assessed in numerical studies."}, {"paperId": "b053b96a2058b0f1b89149e375dbb84e1600cf55", "title": "An introduction to causal mediation analysis", "year": 2024, "citationCount": 20, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "", "status": null, "license": null, "disclaimer": "Notice: The following paper fields have been elided by the publisher: {'abstract'}.
